risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Treatment allocation used a minimization algorithm with a random component [8,10]." Comment: This describes a systematic allocation process that includes a random component, which is sufficient to indicate a proper random sequence generation method for Low risk under the stated permissive criteria.

_evidence.method_quote: Treatment allocation used a minimization algorithm with a random component [8,10].
_evidence.method_summary: Allocation used a minimization algorithm with a random component.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Treatment allocation used a minimization algorithm with a random component [8,10]." Comment: The extracted evidence identifies an explicit allocation method that incorporates a random component and does not indicate an open schedule or other predictable assignment procedure. No signal suggests the recruiter could foresee the next assignment, so this is judged low risk for allocation concealment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Treatment allocation used a minimization algorithm with a random component [8,10].
_evidence.note: The article describes treatment allocation but does not describe any separate concealment procedure; however, it states a minimization algorithm with a random component.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The TTrials are a coordinated group of multi-center, double-blind placebo-controlled trials" and "Participants received AndroGel®, testosterone in an alcohol-water gel, or matched placebo gel, administered transdermally daily, for one year." Comment: The study explicitly states that it was double-blind and placebo-controlled, and it used a matched placebo gel, indicating participants and personnel delivering treatment were intended to be blinded. The reported outcomes were objective imaging and laboratory measures, so knowledge of allocation would be unlikely to influence the dominant outcomes even if blinding were imperfect.

_evidence.blinding_quote: The TTrials are a coordinated group of multi-center, double-blind placebo-controlled trials [8] to evaluate the effects of testosterone treatment in older men with low testosterone levels for no apparent reason other than age, on clinical endpoints that have been shown to improve with testosterone therapy in men with disease-induced hypogonadism.
_evidence.blinding_method: matched placebo gel; "Participants received AndroGel®, testosterone in an alcohol-water gel, or matched placebo gel, administered transdermally daily, for one year."

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Experienced readers in the CCTA core lab blinded to the assigned treatment visually evaluated the CCTA scans for the presence, extent, and severity of coronary artery plaque." Comment: The outcome assessors were an independent CCTA core lab/experienced cardiac CT readers, and they were explicitly blinded to treatment allocation (and also to demographics, randomization and risk factors). The reported outcomes for this trial component are imaging-derived/objective measures (e.g., plaque volume, CAC score, LV mass, stenosis evaluation), so lack of blinding would be unlikely to influence measurement; here blinding was in fact reported, supporting low risk.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Experienced readers in the CCTA core lab blinded to the assigned treatment visually evaluated the CCTA scans for the presence, extent, and severity of coronary artery plaque. ... The CCTA-core lab read all CTA scans, blinded to all demographics, testosterone randomization and risk factors, in near real time (within one week of acquisition) during enrollment and scanning period for stenosis evaluation and assessability. Three level 3 experienced cardiac CT readers evaluated all studies in the trial.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article gives no randomized-arm follow-up counts or CONSORT-style attrition data for the analysed outcome. It only states, "All subjects with a baseline and 12-month plaque volume measurement will be included in the analysis," and reports pre-randomization screening/eligibility numbers ("199 men consented to be screened," "170 were eligible," "165 had a baseline scan") plus "Five men could not be scanned due to high heart rates or lack of venous access." Comment: These data do not show post-randomization loss on the analysed outcome, so the amount and balance of attrition cannot be determined; by Cochrane guidance this is Unclear rather than High or Low.

_evidence.notes: No CONSORT-style per-arm flow for randomized groups was provided. The analysis states: 'All subjects with a baseline and 12-month plaque volume measurement will be included in the analysis.' Reported counts include '199 men consented to be screened,' '170 were eligible,' '165 had a baseline scan,' and 'Five men could not be scanned due to high heart rates or lack of venous access,' but not randomized-arm follow-up numbers.
